did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for the classification of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) from normal skin in full-field optical coherence tomography (FFOCT) images. Specifically, they trained a custom 10-layer CNN from scratch and compared it against pre-trained architectures like VGG-16 and InceptionV3 to achieve a classification accuracy of 95.93%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a custom 10-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to detect Basal Cell Carcinoma in FFOCT images. It details specific methods including data acquisition from 40 images, annotation, preprocessing, and model training, and reports new results such as a classification accuracy of 95.93%. Although published in the proceedings of the IEEE ISBI 2018 symposium, it is a full research article reporting new technical findings rather than a review or abstract.

title: BASAL CELL CARCINOMA DETECTION IN FULL FIELD OCT IMAGES USING CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORKS
journal: 2018 IEEE 15th International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI 2018)
brief_summary: This study investigates the use of full-field optical coherence tomography (FFOCT) for the automated detection of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in skin excisions from Mohs surgery. The authors developed a custom 10-layer convolutional neural network (CNN) trained from scratch on a dataset of 40 high-resolution FFOCT images annotated by a dermatopathologist. To address class imbalance and limited data, the researchers employed data augmentation techniques, including oversampling and geometric transformations, alongside preprocessing steps like Gaussian filtering and standardization. The proposed CNN architecture was evaluated against pre-trained models such as VGG-16 and InceptionV3, which suffered from overfitting due to the small dataset size. The custom CNN achieved a classification accuracy of 95.93%, with a sensitivity of 95.2% and specificity of 96.54%, demonstrating the feasibility of using deep learning for FFOCT-based optical biopsy.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection and classification in the study. The dataset contained 9.5% pathological (BCC) annotated data. The CNN was trained to distinguish BCC patches from normal skin patches.
The negative class used for binary classification. The majority of the annotated data (90.5%) was labeled as normal. The study aimed to discriminate BCC from normal skin.

Mentioned as areas surrounding tumors that were not annotated during training. The model tended to classify these unlabeled abnormal areas as BCC during testing, but they were not a formal class in the training labels.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of image patches into 'Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)' or 'Normal skin' classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is to build a classifier that learns a generalized distribution of the data for two classes: normal and BCC. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (BCC vs. Normal) for 256x256 pixel patches extracted from FFOCT images. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy (95.93%), sensitivity (95.2%), and specificity (96.54%). The loss function used was binary cross-entropy.

datasets_used.name: FFOCT Skin Excision Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset consisting of 40 high-resolution Full-Field Optical Coherence Tomography (FFOCT) images of tissue excisions obtained from Mohs surgery, biopsies, and conventional excisions. The images were acquired using the Light-CT scanner developed by LLTech, France. The samples were manually segmented and diagnosed by a dermatopathologist using the Cytomine platform, with validation against gold standard H&E frozen sections.

use.training: Used to train a custom 10-layer CNN from scratch and compare against pre-trained models (VGG-16, InceptionV3). The dataset was split into patches (108,082 total patches: 59,112 normal, 48,970 BCC), with 80% of patches used for training.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; performance was measured on the remaining 20% of patches.
use.testing: Used to evaluate classification accuracy (95.93%), sensitivity (95.2%), and specificity (96.54%).
use.other: Used for data augmentation (oversampling, flipping, rotation, shifting) to address class imbalance and limited data size.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy (95.93%), sensitivity (95.2%), and specificity (96.54%).

FFOCT Skin Excision Dataset.notes: The pre-trained models (VGG-16 and InceptionV3) suffered from significant overfitting due to the small dataset size (40 images) and class imbalance (9.5% BCC). The custom CNN was trained from scratch to address this. The custom model also tended to classify unlabeled abnormal tissue surrounding tumors as BCC. Results are described as 'raw' but promising.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained from scratch; weights initialized using the Glorot method

architecture_details.feature_extraction: 4 convolutional blocks (each with two convolutional layers) followed by max-pooling and 25% dropout
architecture_details.convolutional_filters: First blocks have 32 filters; subsequent blocks have 64 filters each
architecture_details.receptive_fields: Vary from 7x7 and 5x5 to 3x3 as depth increases
architecture_details.classifier: Two fully-connected layers (512 and 64 neurons) followed by 50% dropout
architecture_details.regularization: Dropout layers (25% after conv blocks, 50% after FC layers)

training_details.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy with class weighting (higher penalization for misclassifying the cancerous class)
training_details.data_augmentation: Oversampling of minority class, horizontal/vertical flipping, slight rotations, and shifts

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of 16-bit DICOM images to 8-bit JPEG format
Application of a 3x3 Gaussian filter for smoothing and speckle noise reduction
Data standardization (zero-centering and normalization) by subtracting the mean intensity and dividing by the standard deviation of the training set
Extraction of 256x256 pixel patches from full-size images
Oversampling of patches with different step values (170px stride for normal class, 40px stride for BCC class) to balance the dataset
Data augmentation via horizontal and vertical flipping, slight rotations, and shifts

data_augmentation_techniques.details: To address class imbalance and augment the dataset, patches were extracted with different stride values: 170px for the normal class and 40px for the BCC class (allowing more overlap). This process doubled the training set size, resulting in 108,082 total patches (59,112 normal and 48,970 BCC).
Horizontal and vertical flipping of patches were applied to generate synthetically augmented images.
Slight rotations and shifts were applied to the patches to further augment the training data.
Class weighting was applied during the computation of the binary cross-entropy loss to penalize misclassification of the under-represented cancerous class more heavily.

conclusions: The study demonstrates the feasibility of using full-field optical coherence tomography (FFOCT) combined with deep learning for the automated detection of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in skin excisions.

A custom 10-layer convolutional neural network (CNN) trained from scratch achieved a classification accuracy of 95.93%, with a sensitivity of 95.2% and specificity of 96.54%, outperforming pre-trained architectures like VGG-16 and InceptionV3 which suffered from overfitting due to the small dataset size.

Pre-trained networks (VGG-16 and InceptionV3) were found to be too complex for the limited data distribution, resulting in lower accuracies of 89.30% and 90.79% respectively, indicating that data over-specification caused overfitting.

The proposed CNN model tends to classify unlabeled abnormal tissue surrounding tumors as BCC, suggesting that the model captures features associated with pathology beyond just the annotated tumor regions.

The results are described as preliminary and raw, highlighting the need for a more consistent dataset, a better understanding of pathologist decision flows, and the inclusion of a third class for unlabeled areas in future work.
Future work intends to adopt a multi-scale approach to capture larger context and to demystify the artificial neural networks to gain further knowledge about the dataset.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset of only 40 FFOCT images, which limits the generalizability of the results.
There is significant class imbalance, with only 9.5% of the annotated data representing Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC).
Pre-trained models (VGG-16 and InceptionV3) suffered from severe overfitting due to the small dataset size and complexity, performing worse than the custom CNN.
The custom 10-layer CNN was trained from scratch to avoid over-specification issues associated with pre-trained architectures on this specific data distribution.
The model tends to classify unlabeled abnormal tissue surrounding tumors as BCC, indicating it may be learning features of pathology beyond just the annotated tumor regions.
Results are described as 'raw' and preliminary; no external validation was performed.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing or confidence intervals were reported; performance metrics are point estimates.

Data leakage risk exists because the same preprocessing (standardization) was applied using training set statistics to test data, and oversampling was used to balance classes, which may inflate performance estimates.
The dataset consists of tissue excisions from Mohs surgery, biopsies, and conventional excisions, which may introduce heterogeneity in sample preparation and imaging conditions.
The study lacks clinical feedback from pathologists to validate the features learned by the network, relying instead on visual inspection of filter activations.
